cards.prompt:
You are running Claude Code headless in CI using the -p/--print flag with --output-format json to review diffs, triage failures, and gate merges. The job must be non-interactive, produce machine-parseable structured results, and minimize false positives that would block good pull requests.

A single Claude Code invocation is prompted to review the diff, triage any failing tests, and decide whether to gate the merge, all in one pass. Reviews are inconsistent and sometimes skip the triage step entirely under time pressure. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Split the pipeline into separate, focused invocations for diff review, failure triage, and merge-gate decision, each with a narrow scope

choices: Retry the single invocation automatically whenever a step appears to be missing
Add a longer system prompt reminding Claude not to skip steps
Increase the context window so the single prompt has more room to cover every task
Split the pipeline into separate, focused invocations for diff review, failure triage, and merge-gate decision, each with a narrow scope
metadata.explanation: Single-responsibility agent design prevents scope overload from causing dropped or inconsistent steps.

cards.prompt:
You are running Claude Code headless in CI using the -p/--print flag with --output-format json to review diffs, triage failures, and gate merges. The job must be non-interactive, produce machine-parseable structured results, and minimize false positives that would block good pull requests.

The headless -p invocation occasionally never returns a final JSON result, instead cycling through tool calls indefinitely until the CI job hits its overall timeout and is killed. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Configure an explicit turn/step budget so the agent is forced to terminate and emit a result

choices: Wrap the invocation in a shell loop that retries it a fixed number of times
Configure an explicit turn/step budget so the agent is forced to terminate and emit a result
Add a cron job that kills and restarts the runner every hour
Increase the CI job's overall timeout so the loop has more time to finish
metadata.explanation: Bounding agent turns/steps prevents unbounded tool-call loops from ever running instead of masking the symptom with a longer timeout.

Two CI jobs reviewing different PRs run Claude Code concurrently on the same self-hosted runner and share a working directory and session cache. Reviews occasionally reference the wrong PR's diff. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Give each CI job an isolated working directory and session so no state is shared between concurrent invocations

choices: Give each CI job an isolated working directory and session so no state is shared between concurrent invocations
Rerun any job whose review looks wrong
Increase the runner's memory so both jobs have more headroom
Add a file lock so only one job can run Claude Code at a time
metadata.explanation: Isolating session/working state per job eliminates the cross-PR contamination at its source rather than serializing or patching around it.

The orchestrator asks Claude to report whether tests pass and gate the merge accordingly, but on one PR it reported 'tests pass' without ever invoking the test-running tool. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Architect the agent so a verdict cannot be produced without a completed tool call to the actual test runner (enforced tool-use before conclusion)

choices: Reword the prompt to more strongly insist that Claude actually run the tests
Lower the model's temperature so it is less likely to guess
Architect the agent so a verdict cannot be produced without a completed tool call to the actual test runner (enforced tool-use before conclusion)
Add a second Claude Code pass to double-check the first one's claim
metadata.explanation: Structurally requiring a real tool call before a verdict can be emitted removes the possibility of hallucinated results, unlike prompt wording alone.

The review agent's MCP toolset includes a 'query-prod-db' tool used to check migration diffs against live data. CI runs occasionally fail or hang because the tool depends on production availability and mutates read counters. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Give the CI agent a read-only/sandboxed or mocked equivalent tool instead of a tool that touches live production data

choices: Add a timeout to the tool call and treat a timeout as a pass
Add a retry loop around the tool call so transient production issues are absorbed
Give the CI agent a read-only/sandboxed or mocked equivalent tool instead of a tool that touches live production data
Catch and ignore any errors the tool call raises
metadata.explanation: CI tools should be designed against sandboxed/mocked dependencies so review reliability doesn't hinge on production availability.

The 'run-tests' tool simply pipes raw pytest stdout back to Claude instead of a structured result, and the agent's --output-format json summary sometimes reports 'passed' after misreading noisy log output as success. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Redesign the tool to return a structured pass/fail/count result instead of raw console text

choices: Redesign the tool to return a structured pass/fail/count result instead of raw console text
Increase the model's context window so it can read the full log more carefully
Have the gate script regex-search the raw output for the word 'passed'
Add a manual reviewer step whenever the log output looks ambiguous
metadata.explanation: Tools that return structured results eliminate the ambiguity of parsing free-form logs, which is the root cause of the misread.

The MCP server exposed to the CI review agent includes a generic 'shell-exec' tool that can run any command, far beyond what's needed to read a diff and post a comment. A misfired instruction once caused it to modify files in the checkout. What is the root-cause fix?

choices: Scope the CI agent's tools to the minimum needed (read diff, read logs, post comment) rather than exposing a general shell-exec tool
Add a prompt instruction telling Claude not to modify files
Add logging so any misuse of shell-exec is visible after the fact
Require a human to approve the PR after any shell-exec call is made
metadata.explanation: Least-privilege tool design prevents the class of failure entirely instead of relying on prompt discipline or after-the-fact detection.

The CI workflow invokes 'claude -p "review this diff"' without the --output-format flag. The parsing script that reads Claude's response to decide pass/fail regularly breaks because the response is prose, not JSON. What is the root-cause fix?

Add --output-format json to the invocation so the response is machine-parseable by design
Ask a human to read the output and enter the verdict manually

A CI job invokes Claude Code without the -p/--print flag. On PRs where Claude would normally ask a clarifying question, the process waits for interactive input and the job hangs until CI kills it. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Invoke Claude Code with -p/--print so it runs fully non-interactively in headless CI

choices: Add a process-level timeout that kills the job after N minutes
Increase the CI job's timeout limit so it has more time to hang before failing
Add a watchdog cron job that restarts hung CI jobs
Invoke Claude Code with -p/--print so it runs fully non-interactively in headless CI

The CI job runs Claude Code with default permissions, giving it broad filesystem and network access well beyond reading diffs and posting comments. A misinterpreted instruction once caused an unintended write outside the repo checkout. What is the root-cause fix?

Run the job only inside an isolated container without changing Claude's permissions
Add logging so unintended actions are caught after the fact

The prompt never defines what counts as a blocking issue, so the agent labels minor style nits as 'blocking' as often as real bugs, and legitimate PRs get gated on trivial complaints. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Add an explicit severity taxonomy and criteria to the prompt so the model can distinguish blocking issues from advisory nits

choices: Add an explicit severity taxonomy and criteria to the prompt so the model can distinguish blocking issues from advisory nits
Have the gate script ignore all findings from the agent
Raise the number of findings required before a PR is blocked

Every CI run passes the entire repository's history and every file into Claude's context rather than just the diff, and larger PRs intermittently fail with context-length errors. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Scope the context sent to Claude to the changed diff and directly relevant files, not the whole repo history

choices: Retry the run automatically whenever a context-overflow error occurs
Truncate the input at a fixed character count chosen at random
Scope the context sent to Claude to the changed diff and directly relevant files, not the whole repo history
Increase the configured context window and hope larger PRs still fit
metadata.explanation: Deliberately scoping context to what's relevant avoids overflow regardless of PR size, unlike widening the window or retrying.

The CI runner has flaky outbound networking, and any transient tool-call failure mid-review currently causes the whole job to fail hard with no recovery, even though the same review usually succeeds on a second try. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Implement retry-with-backoff for tool calls as part of the pipeline's reliability layer

choices: Have engineers manually rerun the CI job whenever it fails
Increase the overall CI job timeout so flaky calls have longer to succeed
Implement retry-with-backoff for tool calls as part of the pipeline's reliability layer
Silently ignore tool-call errors and continue as if they succeeded
metadata.explanation: Built-in retry-with-backoff at the tool-call layer is the standard reliability pattern for absorbing transient network failures.

The CI pipeline architecture always runs all three stages (lint, test, review) to completion regardless of outcome, even when the lint stage already fails in a way that should block the merge outright; the review and test stages still burn several minutes of Claude Code invocation time on a PR that's already doomed to be blocked.
cards.answer: Architect the pipeline to short-circuit and skip later stages once an earlier stage produces a blocking failure, rather than always running every stage to completion

choices: Architect the pipeline to short-circuit and skip later stages once an earlier stage produces a blocking failure, rather than always running every stage to completion
Run all three stages in parallel so the wasted time overlaps instead of stacking
Cache the lint stage's result so a rerun doesn't redo the same work
Reduce the timeout on the review and test stages so they finish faster even when doomed
metadata.explanation: Fail-fast orchestration that short-circuits on a blocking failure eliminates the wasted invocations architecturally, unlike speeding up, parallelizing, or caching work that shouldn't run at all.

When any step in the pipeline fails, the orchestrator retries the entire pipeline from the start, which has caused duplicate PR comments to be posted from earlier successful steps rerunning. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Make each pipeline step idempotent and checkpointed so retries don't redo already-completed side effects

choices: Add a delay before retrying so duplicates are less likely
Add a post-hoc script that deletes duplicate PR comments
Make each pipeline step idempotent and checkpointed so retries don't redo already-completed side effects
Silence the logging for retried steps so duplicates are less visible
metadata.explanation: Idempotent, checkpointed orchestration is the standard fix for duplicate side effects on retry, not cleanup after the fact.

The orchestrator lets the same Claude Code invocation that reviews a PR also merge it automatically whenever it 'deems the change safe,' with no separate approval step. A subtly risky change was auto-merged because the agent was confident but wrong. What is the root-cause fix?

Add a second Claude Code agent to review the first agent's decision before merging
Raise the confidence threshold the agent must report before auto-merging
metadata.explanation: Decoupling the decision agent from the privileged merge action with a human gate addresses the architectural risk, not just tuning confidence.

The orchestrator spawns one Claude Code process per changed file in a PR, so a 200-file refactor PR launches 200 headless invocations, blowing the CI time and cost budget. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Batch review by logical unit (e.g., a single pass over a summarized diff) instead of one invocation per changed file

choices: Batch review by logical unit (e.g., a single pass over a summarized diff) instead of one invocation per changed file
Run the 200 invocations in parallel across more runners to finish faster
Silently cap review to the first N files without telling the author
Increase the per-invocation timeout so each file-level call has more room
metadata.explanation: Batching by logical review unit fixes the orchestration granularity causing the cost blowup, rather than just parallelizing the same excess work.

The 'get-diff' tool's description doesn't specify which git ref the diff is computed against, so on rebase-heavy PRs the agent has reviewed the diff against the wrong base and missed real changes. What is the root-cause fix?

Add more worked examples in the prompt showing which ref to use
Rewrite the tool's schema/description with explicit, unambiguous ref parameters (e.g., base_sha, head_sha)

The 'fetch-ci-logs' tool has no pagination and silently truncates output for large failure logs, so the triage agent has repeatedly missed the actual error buried past the truncation point. What is the root-cause fix?

Increase the model's context window so more of the truncated text fits

Add pagination/complete retrieval support to the log-fetching tool so no content is silently dropped

Redesign the tool to surface failures explicitly (a clear error/status) so the agent can detect and handle them

You are building a pipeline that extracts structured JSON from documents at scale using Claude. Output must conform to a strict schema. The pipeline runs many documents through Message Batches, validates and retries on failure, and must never fabricate values that are absent from the source document.

For documents with genuinely ambiguous values, such as a handwritten total that could be read two ways, Claude confidently reports one interpretation as if certain, giving downstream review no signal that it was a judgment call. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Give the schema a way to represent uncertainty explicitly, such as an optional alternate-value or ambiguity flag, and instruct the model to use it

choices: Instruct the model to always pick the most likely reading
Give the schema a way to represent uncertainty explicitly, such as an optional alternate-value or ambiguity flag, and instruct the model to use it
Retry ambiguous documents with a higher temperature to see if the answer changes
Add a single confidence field applied to the whole document
metadata.explanation: The schema itself needs a structural way to represent genuine ambiguity so downstream consumers can see when a judgment call was made.

cards.prompt:
You are building a pipeline that extracts structured JSON from documents at scale using Claude. Output must conform to a strict schema. The pipeline runs many documents through Message Batches, validates and retries on failure, and must never fabricate values that are absent from the source document.

Every extraction prompt is padded with ten few-shot examples "to be safe," most of which are redundant near-duplicates that don't materially improve accuracy over two or three well-chosen ones, and cost has crept up as a result. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Curate a small set of high-quality, diverse few-shot examples rather than piling on many redundant ones

choices: Enable prompt caching to reduce the cost of the padded prompt
Switch to a cheaper model to offset the extra token cost of the padded prompt
Automatically truncate the examples down to a token budget without curating for quality
Curate a small set of high-quality, diverse few-shot examples rather than piling on many redundant ones
metadata.explanation: Example quality and diversity, not quantity, drive accuracy; curating a small diverse set removes cost without the workarounds of caching or truncation.

A rare class of documents causes extraction to return a valid-looking response that silently omits an entire required top-level section, and the pipeline's validator only checks field types, not the presence of required top-level keys, so these slip through. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Tighten validation logic to check schema completeness, including presence of required top-level keys/structure, not just field-level types

choices: Add a retry whenever the response looks unusually short
Tighten validation logic to check schema completeness, including presence of required top-level keys/structure, not just field-level types
Increase max_tokens to make omission less likely
Add a manual weekly spot-check sample of outputs
metadata.explanation: Validation must check structural completeness, not just field types, to catch entirely missing required sections.

cards.prompt:
You are building a pipeline that extracts structured JSON from documents at scale using Claude. Output must conform to a strict schema. The pipeline runs many documents through Message Batches, validates and retries on failure, and must never fabricate values that are absent from the source document.

The pipeline has no monitoring or alerting on aggregate null-rate or validation-failure-rate across batches, so when documents from a new source format started failing at a 40% rate, nobody noticed for a week because individual failures were retried and logged but no one watched the trend. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Instrument the pipeline with monitoring/alerting on reliability metrics like failure rate and null-rate trends so systemic issues surface proactively

choices: Add a daily manual review of a random sample of outputs
Extend log retention so the data is available if someone eventually looks
Increase the retry count so more individual failures eventually succeed
Instrument the pipeline with monitoring/alerting on reliability metrics like failure rate and null-rate trends so systemic issues surface proactively
metadata.explanation: Proactive monitoring and alerting on aggregate reliability metrics is what surfaces systemic regressions that per-document retry logs bury.

You are equipping developers with Claude Code as a daily driver: built-in tools (read, write, edit, bash, grep, glob) for navigating and changing a codebase, with MCP reserved for genuinely external systems. During a large refactor of the payment module, the developer lets Claude Code start editing files immediately with no upfront plan. Partway through, edits in one file contradict decisions made three files earlier, and the developer has to manually reconcile the mess. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Require the agent to produce and get sign-off on a written task breakdown/plan before any file is edited

choices: Ask the agent to commit after every file edit so mistakes are easier to revert
Increase the context window so the agent can "remember" earlier decisions
Require the agent to produce and get sign-off on a written task breakdown/plan before any file is edited
Run a second cleanup pass with Claude Code after the refactor to fix inconsistencies
metadata.explanation: Planning before execution prevents downstream edits from contradicting earlier decisions, rather than just making the contradictions easier to clean up.

To rename a function across the codebase, the orchestrator dispatches several subagents in parallel, each assigned a different part of the task, but two of them end up writing to the same file at the same time and one subagent's edit silently clobbers the other's. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Partition the work so no two concurrently running subagents ever write to the same file

choices: Have a subagent retry automatically whenever its edit fails to apply
Partition the work so no two concurrently running subagents ever write to the same file
Add a bash-level file lock (e.g., flock) around each subagent's write
Abandon subagents and have a single agent do the entire rename, no matter the size
metadata.explanation: Race conditions in parallel agents come from overlapping write scope; the fix is partitioning file ownership, not bolting on retries or locks.

metadata.explanation: A completion hook guarantees checkpoint writes independent of the coordinator's own logic ever executing, unlike prompt reminders or manual saves.

Add automatic retries against the same down server with longer timeouts.
Pause the entire run and wait for the server to come back online.

Design the orchestration with graceful degradation — fallback data sources or the ability to proceed with partial/lower-confidence results when a dependency is unavailable — rather than a hard, all-or-nothing dependency on one external service.

The "ticket resolution" structured output nests five levels deep (resolution > billing > adjustment > line_item > amount), and the model frequently misplaces sibling fields at the wrong nesting level, corrupting a small but steady percentage of resolutions in ways that are hard to catch. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Flatten and simplify the structured output schema to match how the task is naturally reasoned about, reducing nesting depth so the model can populate it reliably

choices: Increase the max token budget so the model has more room to get the nesting right
Add more few-shot examples showing correctly nested output
Flatten and simplify the structured output schema to match how the task is naturally reasoned about, reducing nesting depth so the model can populate it reliably
Validate the nested output after generation and reject malformed ones
metadata.explanation: Deeply nested schemas are error-prone for models to populate reliably; flattening the schema to match the task's natural structure reduces misplacement errors more than examples, post-hoc validation, or token budget.

Switch to a model with a larger context window
